title: Solving the Rubik's cube with deep reinforcement learning and search
data_sources: Rokicki et al. optimal Rubik's cube solver (PDB, 182 GB)

data_scope.test_set_sokoban: 1000 examples (from standard benchmark with 900000 training examples)

core_conclusion: DeepCubeA solves 100% of all test Rubik's cube configurations, finding a shortest path 60.3% of the time, without domain-specific knowledge. By combining deep approximate value iteration with batch weighted A* search and a reverse-scrambling training curriculum, it uses far less memory than pattern databases and runs over 200x faster than classical solvers on the 24-puzzle. The same algorithm generalizes to six other combinatorial puzzles including Sokoban.
why_it_matters: First domain-knowledge-free method to reliably solve the Rubik's cube at 100%; demonstrates scalable generalization across seven structurally distinct environments; solves the sparse-reward problem via reverse curriculum; opens path to general planning AI in robotics and molecular design; ships as a public web server and Code Ocean capsule.
why_nature: Landmark AI capability result (step change in planning ability); generalizable algorithm not a narrow engineering trick; principled bridge between classical DP/search and deep learning with empirical admissibility analysis; fully open and reproducible.
added_value_to_my_research: GNN replacement for flat DNN enables cross-city transferable accessibility heuristic via reverse-curriculum training
Reverse-curriculum on scrambled service distributions -> fast near-optimal facility placement without MIP solver
Admissibility-analysis workflow -> conformal prediction wrapper for calibrated spatial ML uncertainty
Time-conditioned cost-to-go trained on GPS/mobile data -> dynamic 2SFCA with learnable service-area boundaries
Solution-trace mining for emergent structural regularities -> discovery of functional spatial motifs in urban networks
